danger_signals.visual_form: onscreen hostile entities with distinct character models
danger_signals.meaning: threat to viking health; avoidance or combat required

danger_signals.visual_form: vertical drops and height transitions in level geometry
danger_signals.meaning: damage or death if vikings fall from great heights; three health points per character

danger_signals.visual_form: arrows and other ranged attacks from enemies
danger_signals.meaning: incoming damage; blockable by Olaf's shield or avoidable through movement

danger_signals.visual_form: visual health indicator (three health points per viking)
danger_signals.meaning: character death imminent; level becomes unwinnable if all three vikings die

safety_signals.visual_form: food items that can be carried and consumed
safety_signals.meaning: restores lost health points; survival resource

safety_signals.visual_form: designated exit point at end of level
safety_signals.meaning: safe completion state; all three vikings must reach exit to progress
blocks enemies and projectiles; provides defensive safety mechanism
safe traversal surface; prevents fall damage when maintained

faction_indicators.color_identity: vibrant character colors optimized for television display; distinct per character
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: time-displaced locations across various periods; alien zoo setting indicates captivity context

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: alien design language (implied by 'inter-galactic zoo' context)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Tomator as distinctive boss enemy; alien enemies throughout levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: space-themed environments, alien technology, bizarre locations across time periods

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: solid platforms, visible paths, accessible exit point
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: vikings can move freely; enemies patrol or remain stationary; cooperative puzzle state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: viking character removed from playable area; health depleted to zero
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: gameplay continues with remaining vikings; level becomes progressively unwinnable; forces restart requirement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: level exit inaccessible; barriers blocking progress (locks, heights, gaps)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: cooperative requirement enforced; individual viking skills must be sequentially deployed; item swapping required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: enemies present onscreen in attacking range
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: vikings receive damage; Baleog can counterattack with sword or bow; Olaf can shield block; Erik must evade or bash

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: alien zoo environment, sci-fi/bizarre locations, time-displaced settings
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: vikings must escape through multiple levels; progression toward confronting Tomator; homeward narrative arc

environmental_storytelling.example: level design across 'various periods of time' communicates narrative of vikings scattered through temporal locations; visual environment changes signal narrative progression toward finding way home
environmental visual language of captivity and alien technology contrasts with viking character design; reinforces kidnapping premise and need for escape

Erik's helmet design visually suggests bashing capability; Baleog's sword and bow are visible on character model; Olaf's shield is prominently displayed—abilities are readable from character silhouette
37 levels (42 in Mega Drive/Genesis) escalate puzzle complexity; final confrontation with Tomator as boss enemy; visual and mechanical journey toward homecoming

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: runs faster than other vikings; can jump; can bash through walls and enemies with helmet
kills enemies with sword at close range; attacks from distance with bow and unlimited arrows; can activate switches from afar with bow
blocks enemies and projectiles with shield; uses shield as hang glider for mobility; shield acts as platform for teammates
each viking must use unique skills sequentially; all three must reach exit simultaneously; cooperative dependency enforced; death of any viking requires restart
present onscreen hazards; deal damage on contact; some capable of ranged projectile attacks; can be killed by Baleog's sword or evaded by movement
emperor antagonist; final confrontation required for narrative completion and homeward return

notes: The Lost Vikings uses vibrant color palette (console-hardware-optimized) to create visual distinction between three playable characters despite limited onscreen color budget. World-state communication relies on: (1) character-specific visual ability representation (helmet, sword, shield, speed), (2) environmental platform design as puzzle language, (3) enemy presence as danger indicator, (4) alien/bizarre environment aesthetic as narrative context (captivity, time displacement). Faction identity minimal—focus is on cooperative gameplay mechanics rather than political factions. The game communicates danger primarily through enemy placement, fall hazards, and health depletion visuals. Safety communicated through shield mechanics, health items, and level exit visibility. No traditional faction warfare or political systems present in available source material.
